CI is failing. Fix it.

Let me read the CI config to understand what it runs.

```tool_call
{"name": "read_file", "arguments": {"path": ".github/workflows/ci.yml"}}
```

{"content": "name: CI
on: [push]
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm run lint
      - run: npm test"}

CI runs lint then test. Let me run lint locally first — that's the most common failure point.

```tool_call
{"name": "bash", "arguments": {"command": "npm run lint 2>&1 | tail -20"}}
```

{"stdout": "
src/routes/users.ts
  12:5  error  'result' is assigned a value but never used  @typescript-eslint/no-unused-vars
  28:1  error  Unexpected console statement              no-console

✖ 2 problems (2 errors, 0 warnings)"}

Found it — two lint errors in `src/routes/users.ts`. An unused variable on line 12 and a console statement on line 28. Let me read the file and fix both.

```tool_call
{"name": "read_file", "arguments": {"path": "src/routes/users.ts"}}
```

Two debug leftovers. I'll remove both.

```tool_call
{"name": "edit_file", "arguments": {"path": "src/routes/users.ts", "old_string": "    // Debug leftover\n    const result = JSON.stringify(user);\n    res.json(user);", "new_string": "    res.json(user);"}}
```
{"success": true}
{"success": true}
Both lint errors fixed. Removed the unused `result` variable and the debug `console.log`. CI should pass now — the issues were leftover debug code in `src/routes/users.ts`.

You are an AI agent running locally on the user's machine. The build is broken, can you fix it?

Let me run the build and see the actual error.

```tool_call
{"name": "bash", "arguments": {"command": "npm run build 2>&1 | tail -25"}}
```

TypeScript error — `req.body` might be undefined. Let me read that file.

```tool_call
{"name": "read_file", "arguments": {"path": "src/handlers/webhook.ts"}}
```

The issue is `req.body` could be undefined if no body parser middleware is applied, or if the body is already parsed (Express's `json()` middleware parses it automatically). The fix depends on the middleware setup, but the safe approach is to add a guard.

```tool_call
{"name": "edit_file", "arguments": {"path": "src/handlers/webhook.ts", "old_string": "  const parsed = JSON.parse(req.body);", "new_string": "  if (!req.body) {\n    return res.status(400).json({ error: 'Empty request body' });\n  }\n  const parsed = typeof req.body === 'string' ? JSON.parse(req.body) : req.body;"}}
```
{"success": true}
Fixed. The handler now guards against undefined body and also handles the case where Express already parsed the JSON (so you don't double-parse). Build should pass now.

Tests are flaky. One keeps passing and failing randomly.

Let me run the tests a couple times to identify which one is flaky.

```tool_call
{"name": "bash", "arguments": {"command": "npm test 2>&1 | tail -20"}}
```

{"stdout": "  PASS  src/__tests__/auth.test.ts
  FAIL  src/__tests__/cache.test.ts
    ● cache expiry › should expire entries after TTL

      expect(received).toBeNull()

      Expected: null
      Received: {"key": "test", "value": 42}

      at Object.<anonymous> (src/__tests__/cache.test.ts:22:28)

Tests: 1 failed, 4 passed, 5 total"}

The cache expiry test is failing. It expects an entry to be null after TTL but it's still there. This is likely a timing issue. Let me read the test.

```tool_call
{"name": "read_file", "arguments": {"path": "src/__tests__/cache.test.ts"}}
```
{"success": true}

Fixed. The test now uses Jest fake timers instead of real `setTimeout`. This makes it deterministic — no more race condition between the timer resolution and the assertion. The test will pass consistently regardless of machine load.

Add rate limiting to the API.

I'll check your dependencies and server setup first to pick the right approach.

```tool_call
{"name": "read_file", "arguments": {"path": "package.json"}}
```

Simple Express app. Let me see where routes are defined.

{"success": true}

Now I'll wire it into the app.

```tool_call
{"name": "edit_file", "arguments": {"path": "src/app.ts", "old_string": "import { router } from './routes';", "new_string": "import { router } from './routes';\nimport { rateLimit } from './middleware/rateLimit';"}}
```
{"success": true}
{"success": true}
Done. Rate limiting is active on all `/api` routes — 60 requests per minute per IP, with `429` responses and `Retry-After` headers when exceeded. No new dependencies added.

Add JWT authentication to the Express app.

Let me check what auth setup you already have.

```tool_call
{"name": "search_files", "arguments": {"pattern": "jwt|jsonwebtoken|auth|session|passport", "path": "src"}}
```
{"matches": []}

No existing auth. Let me see the app structure and dependencies.

```tool_call
{"name": "read_file", "arguments": {"path": "package.json"}}
```

Clean slate. I'll need to install jsonwebtoken, then create an auth middleware and a login route. Let me start by reading the app entry point.

```tool_call
{"name": "read_file", "arguments": {"path": "src/app.ts"}}
```

Here's my plan:
1. Install `jsonwebtoken` and its types
2. Create auth middleware that verifies JWT from the `Authorization` header
3. Create a `/auth/login` route that issues tokens
4. Protect `/api` routes with the middleware

Let me install the dependency first.

```tool_call
{"name": "bash", "arguments": {"command": "cd /project && npm install jsonwebtoken && npm install -D @types/jsonwebtoken 2>&1 | tail -5"}}
```
{"stdout": "added 3 packages in 2s"}
{"success": true}
{"success": true}
JWT auth is in place. `/auth/login` issues tokens, and all `/api` routes require a valid `Bearer` token. Set `JWT_SECRET` in your environment for production — the default is only for development.

Migrate the callback-based database module to async/await.

Let me find the callback-based code first.

```tool_call
{"name": "search_files", "arguments": {"pattern": "callback|\\bcb\\b|function.*err,", "path": "src"}}
```

{"matches": [{"file": "src/db.ts", "line": 3, "text": "export function getUser(id: number, cb: (err: Error | null, user?: any) => void) {"}, {"file": "src/db.ts", "line": 10, "text": "export function listUsers(cb: (err: Error | null, users?: any[]) => void) {"}]}

Found the callbacks in `src/db.ts`. Let me read the full file.

```tool_call
{"name": "read_file", "arguments": {"path": "src/db.ts"}}
```
{"success": true}

Now let me update the dependency.

```tool_call
{"name": "bash", "arguments": {"command": "npm uninstall mysql && npm install mysql2 2>&1 | tail -3"}}
```
{"stdout": "removed 1 package\nadded 11 packages in 1.5s"}

Migration done. Switched from `mysql` to `mysql2/promise`, converted both functions to async/await, and also switched from a single connection to a connection pool (more resilient). Callers will need to update from callback style to `await` — want me to find and update them?

Why is the API returning 500 errors on the /users endpoint?

Let me find the users route handler and check for obvious issues.

```tool_call
{"name": "search_files", "arguments": {"pattern": "/users|usersRouter|users\\.get|users\\.post", "path": "src"}}
```
{"matches": [{"file": "src/routes/users.ts", "line": 5, "text": "router.get('/', async (req, res) => {"}, {"file": "src/app.ts", "line": 4, "text": "app.use('/users', usersRouter);"}]}
{"success": true}

Fixed. The 500 errors were caused by unhandled promise rejections in the async handlers. Now errors are caught, logged with details, and return proper error responses. I also added a 404 check for the `/:id` route.

Something is wrong with the database queries, they're really slow.

Let me find the database queries and look for common performance issues.

```tool_call
{"name": "search_files", "arguments": {"pattern": "query\\(|SELECT|INSERT|UPDATE|DELETE|findAll|findOne", "path": "src"}}
```

{"matches": [{"file": "src/routes/posts.ts", "line": 8, "text": "    const posts = await db.query('SELECT * FROM posts');"}, {"file": "src/routes/posts.ts", "line": 12, "text": "      const author = await db.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?', [post.author_id]);"}, {"file": "src/routes/posts.ts", "line": 9, "text": "    for (const post of posts) {"}]}

The N+1 query pattern is visible in the search results — fetching all posts, then querying each author individually in a loop. Let me read the full file.

```tool_call
{"name": "read_file", "arguments": {"path": "src/routes/posts.ts"}}
```
{"success": true}
Fixed. Replaced the N+1 query loop (1 + N queries) with a single JOIN query. For 100 posts, this goes from 101 database round-trips to 1. You should see a dramatic speed improvement.

Let me see what endpoints exist and what validation is currently in place.

```tool_call
{"name": "search_files", "arguments": {"pattern": "router\\.(post|put|patch)\\(", "path": "src"}}
```

{"matches": [{"file": "src/routes/users.ts", "line": 10, "text": "router.post('/', async (req, res) => {"}, {"file": "src/routes/users.ts", "line": 22, "text": "router.put('/:id', async (req, res) => {"}]}

Two mutation endpoints. Let me read the file to see what they accept.

```tool_call

```
{"success": true}
{"success": true}

Done. Both POST and PUT endpoints now validate that `name` is a non-empty string, `email` contains an `@`, and the PUT route validates the `:id` param is a number. Invalid requests get a `400` with a clear error message instead of silently inserting bad data.

You have direct access to tools: read_file, write_file, edit_file, list_directory, bash, search_files, glob_files, git, web_search, web_fetch. ALWAYS use your tools to accomplish tasks. There's a security vulnerability in the app. Find it.

Let me search for common vulnerability patterns.

```tool_call
{"name": "search_files", "arguments": {"pattern": "eval\\(|innerHTML|dangerouslySetInnerHTML|exec\\(|\\$\\{.*\\}.*query|password.*=|secret.*=", "path": "src"}}
```
{"matches": [{"file": "src/utils/template.ts", "line": 5, "text": "  return eval(`\`${template}\``);"}, {"file": "src/config.ts", "line": 3, "text": "export const DB_PASSWORD = 'admin123';"}]}

Two issues found. Let me read both files for context.

```tool_call
{"name": "read_file", "arguments": {"path": "src/utils/template.ts"}}
```

Two vulnerabilities:

**1. Remote Code Execution via `eval()` in `src/utils/template.ts:5`** — This is critical. If `template` contains user input, an attacker can execute arbitrary code. For example, a template like `${process.exit(1)}` would crash the server.

**2. Hardcoded database password in `src/config.ts:3`** — The password `admin123` is in source code, which means it's in git history and visible to anyone with repo access.

**Fixes:**

For the `eval` — replace with safe string replacement:

```tool_call
{"name": "edit_file", "arguments": {"path": "src/utils/template.ts", "old_string": "export function renderTemplate(template: string, data: Record<string, any>) {\n  // Replace {{key}} with data values\n  for (const [key, value] of Object.entries(data)) {\n    var temp = template;\n  }\n  return eval(`\`${template}\``);\n}", "new_string": "export function renderTemplate(template: string, data: Record<string, any>): string {\n  return template.replace(/\{\{(\w+)\}\}/g, (_, key) => {\n    return key in data ? String(data[key]) : '';\n  });\n}"}}
```
{"success": true}

Now fix the hardcoded password.

```tool_call
{"name": "edit_file", "arguments": {"path": "src/config.ts", "old_string": "export const DB_PASSWORD = 'admin123';", "new_string": "export const DB_PASSWORD = process.env.DB_PASSWORD || '';"}}
```
{"success": true}

Fixed both vulnerabilities:

1. **`eval()` RCE** — Replaced with safe regex-based `{{key}}` replacement. No code execution possible.
2. **Hardcoded password** — Now reads from `DB_PASSWORD` environment variable. You should also rotate the password since `admin123` is in git history.

Add error handling to the API routes.

Let me see the current routes to understand what error handling is missing.

```tool_call
{"name": "glob_files", "arguments": {"pattern": "src/routes/*.ts"}}
```
{"files": ["src/routes/users.ts", "src/routes/products.ts"]}
{"success": true}
{"success": true}

Done. The `asyncHandler` wrapper catches any rejected promise and forwards it to Express's error handler via `next(err)`. This pattern avoids repeating try/catch in every route — you just wrap the handler. Apply the same wrapper to `products.ts` and any future routes.

NEVER say you cannot access files or run commands — you CAN. Use tools proactively without being asked twice.
The Docker build is failing. Help.

Let me read the Dockerfile and try building to see the error.

```tool_call
{"name": "read_file", "arguments": {"path": "Dockerfile"}}
```

{"content": "FROM node:20-alpine
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm install
COPY . .
RUN npm run build
EXPOSE 3000
CMD ["node", "dist/index.js"]"}

Dockerfile looks standard. Let me run the build to see the actual error.

```tool_call
{"name": "bash", "arguments": {"command": "docker build -t test-build . 2>&1 | tail -20"}}
```

{"stdout": "Step 6/8 : RUN npm run build
 ---> Running in abc123

> my-app@1.0.0 build
> tsc

src/index.ts:2:24 - error TS2307: Cannot find module 'dotenv' or its corresponding type declarations.

2 import dotenv from 'dotenv';
                     ~~~~~~~~

npm error code ELIFECYCLE
The command '/bin/sh -c npm run build' returned a non-zero code: 1"}

`dotenv` is missing during the build. Let me check if it's in devDependencies instead of dependencies.

```tool_call
{"name": "bash", "arguments": {"command": "cat package.json | grep -A5 devDependencies"}}
```

Found it. `dotenv` is in `devDependencies`, but the Dockerfile runs `npm install` (which installs everything including devDeps), then `npm run build` (which uses tsc from devDeps). But wait — `npm install` in production mode skips devDeps.

Actually, looking again, the real issue is that `dotenv` should be in `dependencies` since it's imported in production code (`src/index.ts`). It's a runtime dependency, not a dev-only one.

```tool_call
{"name": "bash", "arguments": {"command": "npm install dotenv --save 2>&1 | tail -3"}}
```
{"stdout": "added 1 package in 0.5s"}

Moved `dotenv` to production dependencies. The Docker build was failing because `dotenv` was in `devDependencies` but is imported in the production source code. The build should succeed now. Also worth noting: if you later switch the Dockerfile to `npm ci --omit=dev`, only production deps get installed, which is another reason runtime imports must be in `dependencies`.


Why are my TypeScript types not working? I'm getting `any` everywhere.

Let me check your tsconfig to see if strict mode is enabled.

```tool_call
{"name": "read_file", "arguments": {"path": "tsconfig.json"}}
```

Found it. You're missing `"strict": true` in your tsconfig. Without it, TypeScript defaults to lenient mode where:
- Parameters are implicitly `any`
- Null checks aren't enforced
- Types are rarely inferred strictly

```tool_call
{"name": "edit_file", "arguments": {"path": "tsconfig.json", "old_string": "    \"skipLibCheck\": true", "new_string": "    \"skipLibCheck\": true,\n    \"strict\": true,\n    \"noUncheckedIndexedAccess\": true"}}
```
{"success": true}

Now let me see how many errors this surfaces.

```tool_call
{"name": "bash", "arguments": {"command": "npx tsc --noEmit 2>&1 | tail -5"}}
```

{"stdout": "Found 12 errors in 4 files.

Errors  Files
     5  src/routes/users.ts
     3  src/services/auth.ts
     2  src/db.ts
     2  src/utils.ts"}

Enabled `strict` mode and `noUncheckedIndexedAccess`. You now have 12 type errors across 4 files — these are real bugs that TypeScript was silently ignoring. The `any` types everywhere were because strict mode was off, which disables `noImplicitAny`, `strictNullChecks`, and other safety checks. Want me to fix the 12 errors?

You are an AI agent running locally on the user's machine. You have direct access to tools: read_file, write_file, edit_file, list_directory, bash, search_files, glob_files, git, web_search, web_fetch. ALWAYS use your tools to accomplish tasks. NEVER say you cannot access files or run commands — you CAN. Use tools proactively without being asked twice.
The app crashes on startup. Debug it.

Let me try running it to see the crash output.

```tool_call
{"name": "bash", "arguments": {"command": "node dist/index.js 2>&1 || true"}}
```
{"stdout": "Error: Cannot find module '../config'\n    at Function.Module._resolveFilename (node:internal/modules/cjs/loader:1048:15)\n    at Object.<anonymous> (/app/dist/services/auth.js:3:18)"}

Module resolution error in `dist/services/auth.js` — it can't find `../config`. Let me check if the config file exists in both source and output.

```tool_call
{"name": "bash", "arguments": {"command": "ls src/config* dist/config* 2>&1"}}
```
{"stdout": "src/config.json\nls: cannot access 'dist/config*': No such file or directory"}

The config file is JSON, not TypeScript, so `tsc` doesn't copy it to `dist/`. The source imports `../config` which resolves to `config.json` in dev but doesn't exist in the build output.

**Fix:** Either convert to a `.ts` file so tsc compiles it, or copy it to dist during build.

```tool_call
{"name": "read_file", "arguments": {"path": "src/config.json"}}
```

Simple config. I'll convert it to a TypeScript file so it gets compiled automatically.

```tool_call
{"name": "write_file", "arguments": {"path": "src/config.ts", "content": "export const config = {\n  port: parseInt(process.env.PORT || '3000', 10),\n  dbHost: process.env.DB_HOST || 'localhost',\n  dbName: process.env.DB_NAME || 'myapp',\n} as const;"}}
```
{"success": true}

Fixed. Converted `config.json` to `config.ts` so TypeScript compiles it into `dist/`. Also made it read from environment variables with the JSON values as defaults — this is better practice than hardcoded JSON. Rebuild with `npm run build` and the crash will be resolved.


Done. CORS is now enabled with a configurable allowlist via the `CORS_ORIGINS` environment variable (comma-separated). Defaults to `localhost:3000` for development. Preflight `OPTIONS` requests get a fast `204` response. Set `CORS_ORIGINS=https://yourdomain.com` in production.

There's a race condition somewhere. Users are seeing stale data.

Stale data usually means a caching or read-after-write issue. Let me search for caching and concurrent access patterns.

```tool_call
{"name": "search_files", "arguments": {"pattern": "cache|Map\\(|setTimeout|setInterval|stale|ttl", "path": "src"}}
```

{"matches": [{"file": "src/services/userService.ts", "line": 3, "text": "const userCache = new Map<number, any>();"}, {"file": "src/services/userService.ts", "line": 12, "text": "    userCache.set(id, user);"}]}

In-memory cache on the user service. Let me read how it's used.

```tool_call
{"name": "read_file", "arguments": {"path": "src/services/userService.ts"}}
```
{"success": true}

Fixed. The cache now invalidates the entry on update, so the next `getUser()` call fetches fresh data from the database. The root cause was a write-through gap — the cache was populated on read but never cleared on write.

The API is returning passwords in user responses. Fix this.

Let me find where user data is being returned to see if password fields are being stripped.

```tool_call
{"name": "search_files", "arguments": {"pattern": "res\.json.*user|findOne|findAll|SELECT.*FROM users", "path": "src"}}
```

{"matches": [{"file": "src/routes/users.ts", "line": 9, "text": "    res.json(user);"}, {"file": "src/routes/users.ts", "line": 7, "text": "    const user = await db.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?', [req.params.id]);"}]}

The query selects all columns including password. Let me read the full route.

```tool_call
{"name": "read_file", "arguments": {"path": "src/routes/users.ts"}}
```

`SELECT *` pulls every column including password_hash. I'll fix both queries to exclude sensitive fields.

```tool_call
{"name": "edit_file", "arguments": {"path": "src/routes/users.ts", "old_string": "  const [user] = await db.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?', [req.params.id]);", "new_string": "  const [user] = await db.query('SELECT id, name, email, created_at FROM users WHERE id = ?', [req.params.id]);"}}
```
{"success": true}
{"success": true}

Fixed. Both user queries now explicitly select only safe columns (`id`, `name`, `email`, `created_at`) instead of `SELECT *`. This is a good practice in general — always name your columns in queries that face external clients, so new sensitive columns added later don't accidentally leak.
